1: The report of Gentile reception of the word (edexanto ta ethnē ton logon tou theou) — phrased as a surprising success — reaches the Jerusalem community before Peter does. The church's news network is already active: what happens in Caesarea is known in Judea before Peter returns to Jerusalem. The risen Lord's mission operates through a connected community that reports, debates, and discerns together — not through isolated apostolic decision-making.
2: 'Those of the circumcision party' (hoi ek peritomēs) — the first appearance of the term that will recur as a technical label for Jewish Christians insisting on circumcision as a requirement for Gentile salvation (Galatians 2:12; Titus 1:10). Their critique — 'you went to uncircumcised men and ate with them' — focuses on the table fellowship rather than the baptism, because table fellowship was the daily concrete marker of Jewish-Gentile distinction. Peter ate at Cornelius's table; this is the charge.
3: The charge of eating with uncircumcised men is precisely the purity boundary the rooftop vision had addressed. The circumcision party's objection is coherent within pre-Pentecost categories; what they lack is the knowledge of the divine orchestration Peter is about to narrate. The risen Lord's method is not decree but narration: he works through events that must be told and re-told until their implications are understood. Peter's account (Acts 11:4–17) is the first of three tellings of the Cornelius story in Acts (Acts 10:1–48; Acts 11:4–17; Acts 15:7–11).
4: Peter narrates 'in order' (kathexēs) — a Lukan term for orderly, reliable narration (cf. Luke 1:3). The orderly account is not merely narrative courtesy but theological method: the risen Lord's actions must be traced in their sequence so that the divine initiative is visible in the pattern. To understand why Peter entered a Gentile home, one must understand the angel (Acts 11:13–14), the rooftop vision (Acts 11:5–10), and the Spirit's command (Acts 11:12) — the chain of divine direction that brought him there.
5: The rooftop vision retold — the sheet descending from heaven with all kinds of animals. Peter's repetition of the vision for the Jerusalem community ensures that the Cornelius decision is not Peter's innovation but the Lord's instruction. The risen Christ's commands are not private revelations to be kept by the recipient but public accounts to be narrated to the community for discernment. Peter's transparency with the Jerusalem community is the model of apostolic accountability.
6: Peter's gaze into the vision — 'looking at it closely, I observed' (atenisas katenooun) — establishes his active engagement rather than passive reception. The fourfold categories of animals (four-footed, wild, reptiles, birds) are the full taxonomy of the Mosaic clean/unclean system. What Peter saw comprehensively was the entire system of ritual purity that governed Jewish-Gentile relations — the vision's scope matched the scope of the practice it was annulling.
7: The divine voice's command — 'Rise, Peter; kill and eat' — retold for the Jerusalem audience. The first repetition (Acts 10:13) was private revelation; the second repetition (Acts 11:7) is public testimony. The command 'Rise' (anastas) before eating connects the vision's content to the resurrection pattern: new life in Christ requires rising above the old boundaries. What death could not separate (Jew and Gentile from God's love) the risen Lord's command now concretely bridges.
8: Peter's three-time refusal retold — 'nothing common or unclean has ever entered my mouth.' His narration of his own resistance is self-incriminating: he confesses that he refused the Lord three times before complying. This honest narration of his own failure mirrors his honest narration of his three denials in the passion (Luke 22:54–62). The apostle who denied Jesus three times and then obeyed three times in the rooftop vision is a man formed by the pattern of failure and restoration that characterized his entire discipleship.
9: 'What God has made clean, do not call common' — the divine declaration retold for Jerusalem. The authority behind the purity revision is God himself, not Peter's theological reasoning. The Jerusalem community is not being asked to accept Peter's opinion but to hear the Lord's declaration. The risen Christ's word, delivered through the Spirit, through the vision, through the narration, carries the same authority as his earthly teaching — the word that once came through the incarnate mouth now comes through the apostolic witness.
10: The three-fold repetition retold — 'This happened three times, and all was drawn up again into heaven.' The three-fold pattern in Peter's story carries the same weight as the three-fold restoration in John 21. The Lord who three times asked 'do you love me?' and restored Peter's apostolic commission three times now three times commands the crossing of the purity boundary. Peter needed three repetitions of both — the dense pattern of threes in his story is the risen Lord's way of reaching through a particular apostle's particular resistance.
11: 'At that very moment three men arrived at the house where we were, sent to me from Caesarea.' The divine timing — vision concludes, men arrive immediately — is the risen Lord's signature: the meaning of the vision is provided by what immediately follows it. The Spirit who spoke in Acts 11:12 had arranged both vision and visitors simultaneously, so that the vision's meaning would be inescapable. This is Jesus's pastoral method extended into the post-resurrection mission: he does not explain abstractly but orchestrates concretely.
12: 'The Spirit told me to go with them, making no distinction.' The crucial phrase — 'making no distinction' (mēden diakrinanta) — is the direct command the Spirit issued. The Spirit who inspired the prophets and fell at Pentecost commands Peter to cross the boundary without hesitation or mental reservation. The same Spirit who 'apportioned to each one individually' the gifts (1 Corinthians 12:11) and 'helps us in our weakness' (Romans 8:26) here commands the most radical social act of Peter's apostolic ministry. The Spirit's authority backs the crossing.
13: Peter narrates the angel's appearance to Cornelius — how the angel stood in his house and commanded him to send for Simon Peter at Joppa. The double divine initiative — angel to Cornelius, Spirit to Peter — is the pattern of the risen Lord's coordinated mission. No one reached Cornelius through their own initiative; no one reached Peter through Cornelius's messengers alone. Both were reached by the Lord simultaneously, each prepared for the encounter the other needed.
14: 'He will declare to you a message by which you will be saved, you and all your household' — the angel's promise to Cornelius includes the household. The scope of salvation — 'you and all your household' — is the Cornelius family's version of the Philippian jailer's promise (Acts 16:31) and connects to Jesus's declaration at Zacchaeus's home: 'Today salvation has come to this house' (Luke 19:9). The household is the unit of mission; the Lord reaches the community through the leader who responds in faith.
15: 'As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell on them just as on us at the beginning.' Peter's phrase 'as I began to speak' establishes the sovereignty of the Spirit's timing: Peter had not completed his sermon, not extended an invitation, not prayed for them. 'Just as on us at the beginning' (hōsper kai eph' hēmas en archē) connects the Gentile Pentecost to the Jewish Pentecost as structurally identical events. The same Lord gave the same gift at the beginning (Acts 2) and now at this new beginning of the Gentile mission.
16: Peter recalls the word of the Lord from Acts 1:5: John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit. This is the only place in Acts where Peter explicitly quotes a word of the Lord Jesus — the risen Lord's pre-ascension promise. The risen Jesus's words, remembered in the moment of divine action, provide the interpretive key: what is happening in Caesarea is the fulfillment of Jesus's own promise about the Spirit's coming.
17: 'If then God gave the same gift to them as he gave to us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I that I could stand in God's way?' — Peter's conclusion is logic from divine action: God gave; therefore I cannot withhold. The argument's structure prevents any human gatekeeping of the Spirit's movement. 'Who was I?' is the apostle's confession of his own subordination to the Lord's initiative — the same subordination Peter had failed to show in denying Jesus and refused to show in his initial resistance to the rooftop vision. The fully formed Peter submits to the Lord's action rather than his own purity convictions.
18: The Jerusalem community falls silent and then glorifies God, concluding: 'Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance that leads to life.' The silence, then praise, is the right response to an account of divine initiative. The conclusion they draw ('God has granted' — not Peter has decided) preserves the divine sovereignty of the moment: the Gentile mission is not a human policy decision but a divine gift. 'Repentance leading to life' (tēn metanoian eis zōēn) — the full phrase of the apostolic proclamation connects Gentile conversion to the life that comes through the risen Christ.
19: The narrative returns to the Hellenist diaspora (from Acts 8:4) — scattered by the persecution after Stephen's martyrdom and speaking the word only to Jews. The gospel is moving along two tracks simultaneously: the Peter-Cornelius track (official apostolic crossing of the Gentile boundary) and the informal Hellenist track (unnamed missionaries reaching Gentiles ahead of the official decision). The risen Lord's mission is not solely through official apostolic channels but through scattered believers following their own Spirit-led initiative.
20: 'But there were some of them, men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who on coming to Antioch spoke to the Hellenists also, preaching the Lord Jesus.' The decisive step — speaking to Hellenists (Greek-speaking Gentiles) about the Lord Jesus — is taken not by Peter or the Twelve but by unnamed diaspora Jewish Christians from Cyprus and Cyrene. The Lord Jesus (Kyrion Iēsoun) as the content of their proclamation names the risen Christ by his full messianic title. The un-commissioned missionaries proclaim the same Lord as the commissioned apostles.
21: 'And the hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number who believed turned to the Lord.' The 'hand of the Lord' — a phrase of divine empowerment in the OT (Exodus 9:3; Joshua 4:24; 1 Kings 18:46; Isaiah 41:10) — rests on the informal Antioch mission. The Lord whose resurrection is the mission's foundation empowers the mission through both official (Peter) and unofficial (Cypriots and Cyreneans) channels. 'Turned to the Lord' (epistrepsen epi ton kyrion) — conversion is explicitly to Jesus as Lord, not to a religion or institution.
22: Jerusalem sends Barnabas to Antioch — the same delegation pattern used to send Peter and John to Samaria (Acts 8:14). The Jerusalem community's oversight of new mission developments is not controlling but confirmatory: they send encouragers and teachers, not inspectors. Barnabas — 'son of encouragement' (Acts 4:36) — is the ideal envoy: his character matches his mission. Jesus had sent out the seventy-two in pairs with the authority to receive hospitality and proclaim peace (Luke 10:1–9); Barnabas's Antioch mission continues this pattern.
23: 'When he came and saw the grace of God, he was glad and exhorted them all to remain faithful to the Lord with steadfast purpose.' Barnabas 'sees' the grace of God — recognizing divine action in what he observes rather than evaluating by his own criteria. What he saw was the mixed Jewish-Gentile community worshipping the risen Lord Jesus. His response is joy (echárē) — the appropriate response to divine gift. His exhortation to 'remain faithful to the Lord with steadfast purpose' echoes Jesus's parable of the soils: the good soil bears fruit with 'a noble and good heart, holding fast' (Luke 8:15).
24: 'For he was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and of faith. And a great many people were added to the Lord.' Barnabas's character portrait — 'full of the Holy Spirit and of faith' — matches the description of Stephen (Acts 6:5) and the qualification for the Seven (Acts 6:3). The result of his Spirit-filled ministry: a great many added to the Lord (prosetethe ochlos hikanos tō kyriō). The Lord is both the source (the Spirit empowering the mission) and the destination (persons added to him) of the Antioch community's growth.
25: Barnabas goes to Tarsus to find Saul — the act that changes the entire subsequent trajectory of Acts. Barnabas's search for Saul (who had been sent to Tarsus after his Damascus-Jerusalem ministry, Acts 9:30) brings back into the narrative the man who will become the primary agent of the Gentile mission. The Spirit-filled encourager's discernment — that Saul's gifts are needed in Antioch — mirrors the community's discernment in Acts 6 that appointed gifts should match ministry need.
26: 'And in Antioch the disciples were first called Christians' (Christianous). The naming of the community by its Lord's title — Christ-ians — is the most theologically precise possible description: these are people defined by Jesus as the Christ. Whether the name was self-chosen, given by outsiders, or divinely designated (the verb chrematisai can indicate divine naming in Greek literature), it anchors the community's identity exclusively in Christ. The Antioch community, formed by the preaching of the Lord Jesus (Acts 11:20), is now publicly named by that Lord's messianic identity.
27: Prophets come from Jerusalem to Antioch — the gift of prophecy crossing the geographical distance between the mother church and the daughter church. The prophetic ministry in Antioch connects to Jesus's promise that the Spirit would guide the community into all truth (John 16:13) and enable testimony before rulers (Luke 12:11–12). The presence of Jerusalem prophets in Antioch demonstrates that the Spirit's gifts are not geographically contained; they flow between communities as the Lord distributes them.
28: Agabus's prophecy of a great famine — fulfilled under Claudius (c. AD 46–47) — is the Spirit's forward vision enabling the community to prepare relief before crisis arrives. The prophetic gift functions not merely for individual guidance but for communal action in advance of historical events. Jesus had warned his disciples of coming sufferings to prepare them (Luke 21:10–11); the Spirit's prophecy through Agabus extends the same provision of advance warning into the post-Pentecost community.
29: Each disciple — 'according to his ability' (ekastos autōn) — determines to send relief to 'the brothers living in Judea.' The voluntary proportional giving mirrors the principle Jesus articulated in the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:15) and that Paul would later formalize: 'each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver' (2 Corinthians 9:7). The Antioch community's generosity toward Judea enacts the koinonia (fellowship) that the risen Lord's name creates across geographical distance.
30: 'This they did, sending it to the elders by the hand of Barnabas and Saul.' The collection sent to the Jerusalem elders through Barnabas and Saul is the first material expression of Gentile-Jewish solidarity in Acts. The pattern of the collection — Gentile communities sending financial relief to the Jewish mother church — will become the defining project of Paul's third missionary journey (Romans 15:25–28; 1 Corinthians 16:1–4; 2 Cor 8–9). The risen Lord's body expressing solidarity across ethnic and geographical lines is the practical shape of the gospel's claim that he is Lord of all.
